Risks

Although IDVO has a higher yield and saw increased dividend growth year-over-year, their options strategy caps their upside. Moreover, compared to SCHY whose dividends were mostly qualified, IDVO only makes sense inside a non-taxable account.

Additionally, they saw high portfolio turnover of 104% in 2024. As a result, ETFs with high turnover rates are subject to higher taxes and transaction costs, resulting in higher taxes if held in anything other than a non-taxable account.

Conclusion

If you're looking to diversify outside the U.S. for the sake of diversification, then SCHY and VYMI may be better options. However, if you're looking for diversification while also focused on income, then IDVO's higher yield and better growth suits you.

Moreover, if you're of age, then Amplify's International Enhanced Dividend Income ETF is the better choice since most of their dividends are considered ordinary income, or capital gains.

Additionally, IDVO's total returns have been strong since inception, beating the S&P and Schwab's International Dividend Equity ETF since late 2022.

And although the U.S. could continue to be the dominant world power and outperform international-based equities for the foreseeable future, IDVO not only offers diversification, but a solid & growing income stream for dividend investors.

data.title: Johnson Controls: Margin Expansion Priced In
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I rate Johnson Controls as Hold with a target price range of $101–110. While the company continues to deliver solid operational performance and margin improvement, most of these fundamentals are already priced in after a 60% rally since mid-2024. At 30x forward P/E and 21x EV/EBITDA, JCI trades at a premium to peers and its own history. With limited upside and macro/competitive risks still in play, I see a more balanced risk/reward profile at current levels.

Introduction

Johnson Controls International is a global leader in technologies for smart, healthy and energy-efficient buildings. The company, which is headquartered in Cork (Ireland), operates in more than 150 countries. Johnson Controls was founded in 1885 in the USA as Johnson Electric Service Company and initially produced automatic temperature control systems in buildings. In 2016, the company merged with Tyco International plc, as a result of which Tyco was renamed Johnson Controls International plc.

In recent years, Johnson Controls has been actively optimizing its portfolio. In the fourth quarter of fiscal year 2024 (ended September 30, 2024), JCI completed the sale of the Air Distribution Technologies division (a certain part of Global Products) and announced an agreement with Bosch to sell the entire Residential and Light Commercial (R&LC) HVAC (Home and Light Commercial HVAC Systems) business. Thus, over the past year, the company has made a portfolio turnaround, getting rid of non-core areas and focusing on the commercial segment.

Balanced Model Between Products and Services

Johnson Controls builds its business around the integration of hardware and service solutions for buildings. The company operates through four geographic segments: Building Solutions North America, Building Solutions EMEA/LA, Building Solutions Asia Pacific, and Global Products. The three "Building Solutions" units focus on HVAC systems, controls and automation, fire and security installations and maintenance. Global Products handles development, manufacturing, and sales of HVAC equipment (under YORK), refrigeration, controls, fire safety, and security products. The company also develops digital offerings such as the OpenBlue platform to support building efficiency and safety.

In 2024, products accounted for about 70% of total revenue, while services contributed 30%. The company aims to grow the share of recurring service revenue over time, supported by digitalization and service contracts. Product margins remain high (Global Products EBITA margin at 30% in Q2 FY2025), while service margins are lower but steadily improving due to scale and mix. The strategy focuses on expanding in attractive markets like data centers, sustainable construction, healthy buildings, and smart infrastructure—mainly through innovation and bundled offerings. The operating model leverages a global sales network, significant R&D spending, and a growing installed base.

Johnson Controls operates in the niche of commercial technologies for buildings: HVAC systems, building automation, security and fire protection systems. The estimate of the total market depends on the source, but even a narrow segment of "building automation" is estimated at approximately $100 billion in 2024. The main competitors are major international HVAC/automation players: Honeywell, Siemens (Smart Infrastructure), Schneider Electric, Carrier (Carrier Global University), Trane Technologies, Daikin, Lennox and Chinese (Midea, Gree). Product competition, especially in HVAC and security, is quite strong: The market is fragmented, there are local OEMs and system integrators. The competition in the services segment is even more dispersed, but the availability of a wide range of service coverage and digital solutions plays a key role. A strong brand portfolio and global sales network give JCI a competitive advantage, but the growth rate of profits and shares depends on the ability to keep the price in the face of increasing competition.

Johnson Controls builds a balanced model with an emphasis on a combination of hardware and repeatable service, which supports sustainable margins. The company is actively moving towards digitalization and strengthening its position in high-margin segments, but remains dependent on the competitive and fragmented HVAC market. Despite the strong operating position and global coverage, further profitability growth will depend on the ability to maintain price and scale service revenue. This imposes restrictions on the growth rate and requires high discipline in the implementation of the strategy.

Steady Performance and Upgraded Outlook

The released report for the second quarter of fiscal year 2025 confirmed the growth of the company's operating indicators.

In the second quarter of fiscal year 2025, Johnson Controls recorded consolidated revenue of $5,676 million, an increase of 1% year-on-year with solid organic growth of 7%. The driver was the Building Solutions platform: North America added 6% (organically +7%) due to demand for Applied HVAC and control systems; EMEA/Latin America grew by 2% (organically +5%), where prices and services raised the result; Asia-Pacific region added 10% (organically +13%) amid the recovery of projects and services. The total order portfolio of Building Solutions reached a record $14.0 billion, growing by 12% organically and providing the company with a good margin of revenue for the coming periods.

Johnson Controls' operational efficiency has strengthened: total adjusted EBITA by segment reached $946 million, with margin growth to 16.7% from 15% a year earlier. Adjusted EPS increased by 19% to $0.82, reflecting net operating performance without the impact of one-time expenses, while GAAP EPS was $0.71. Net income from continuing operations reached $473 million, driven by increased profitability and moderate interest expenses. The steady margin growth confirms the current strategic vector: the focus on more profitable service areas, the exit from low-margin assets and strict pricing discipline, which, combined with a record order portfolio, creates a solid foundation for further development.

After Q2, the company raised its forecast for 2025. Management expects organic revenue growth in the mid-single-digits range and an improvement in adjusted EBITA margin of approximately 90 basis points by the end of the year. Adjusted EPS increased to $3.60 (previously $3.50–$3.60). The new CEO dismisses signs of slowing demand and sees a strong supply of orders, especially for services and systems in NA. Given the record backlog (+12%) and the continued recovery in the global economy, current forecasts look realistic or even conservative. However, the raising of the EPS forecast and the tightening of cost reduction directions indicate the confidence of the management.

Taking into account the management's forecast for the entire 2025 financial year, which assumes mid-single-digit organic revenue growth and an expansion of adjusted EBITA margin by 90 bps, the decline in the third quarter looks rather temporary. According to analysts' consensus, revenue in Q3 2025 is expected to reach $5.99 billion (-17.2% YoY), which is primarily due to the high base of last year, when there was still an undivided R&LC HVAC segment in the structure. The exclusion of this area from the portfolio and the base effect distort the annual dynamics, not reflecting the deterioration of operational trends. The seasonal nature of demand is also having an impact, as the implementation of some orders is shifting closer to the fourth quarter. Against this background, I agree with management's position: current expectations of a decline in Q3 do not contradict the overall improvement in annual performance.

The debt burden of Johnson Controls is controllable. The Debt/EBITDA ratio is about 2.86x, and the Net Debt/EBITDA is about 2.63x. JCI did not take out new loans in Q2, repaid some of the old bonds and is preparing to use the proceeds of $5 billion from the sale of R&LC HVAC for early repayment of debt. Thus, the debt burden does not pose a threat: Even after launching a large buyback program, the company will maintain a moderate debt burden.

Hold: Rich Pricing, Modest Return Outlook

Since the summer of 2024, Johnson Controls International shares have shown impressive growth of almost +60%, more than quadrupling the dynamics of the S&P 500 index over the same period. This gap was largely due to strong quarterly results, margin growth, and expectations from portfolio reorganization. However, by now, a significant part of the fundamental improvements has already been reflected in the price: The growth momentum is gradually fading.

My estimate is based on a DCF model with an unlevered FCF plan for 2025–2030. I envision an average annual revenue growth of 7.7%, from $19.6 billion in 2025 to $28.5 billion by 2030, with a stable operating margin of about 11%, which increases EBIT to $3.1 billion. I keep capex at 2.1–2.2% of sales, and I consider the need for net working capital to be close to zero, since the dynamics fluctuate for 0.1% of revenue. Against this background, the company is expected to generate free flow from $2.4 billion in 2025 to $3.3 billion in 2030, which corresponds to a stable FCF yield of about 11–12% per year, comparable to the historical average.

The model uses a WACC rate of 9.15%, the prerequisites are shown in the table above.

Current multiples of JCI shares are at a high-level relative to the market. Forward P/E is about 30x (significantly higher than the sector's 22x) and Forward EV/EBITDA is 21x (versus 11x for the industry). Price/Sales is 3x, which is almost twice the sector average (1.5–2x). The multipliers show that stocks are trading at a premium over the historical average (average P/E over 5 years is 21x). This means that there is limited potential for a market acceleration of stocks without significantly exceeding forecasts. Most of the growth is probably already embedded in the current price.

The results of the valuation of Johnson Controls shares using the P/E and EV/EBITDA methods show that the securities are already close to their fair range. According to the P/E multiplier, at the target level of 29.0x and the required yield of 9.2–10.2%, the shares give a neutral rating ranging from -3.9% to +1.0%, and at 31.0x — a maximum of +6.4%, which also does not look convincing. The EV/EBITDA method shows a more positive picture only in the high valuation scenario (23.0x), where an upside of up to +20.2% is possible, but this requires aggressive assumptions and a higher-historical multiplier. Based on this, I have a Hold rating with a target price in the range of $101–110.

Buybacks Accelerate, Dividends Stay Steady

Johnson Controls has traditionally pursued an active policy of returning capital to shareholders through dividends and buybacks. On dividends: the JCI Board of Directors regularly announces quarterly payments (for July 2025 – $0.37 per share), which is equivalent to $1.48 per year. Dividends have been paid consistently since 1887, and the company has raised the rate annually over the past five years (for example, $0.37 was announced in October 2024, and the plan is also in July 2025). Thus, the current dividend yield is about 1.4–1.5% (at a price of $105), and the payout policy is stable and conservative.

As for buyback, JCI is significantly increasing buyback. On June 13, 2025, the Board of Directors approved an additional announcement of $9 billion for share repurchase (in addition to the existing unused balance of $1.1 billion). This new authorization does not have a deadline and assumes that it will be repurchased at a market price through accelerated programs. The company has almost doubled the buyback potential to $10 billion, which is equivalent to almost half of JCI's capitalization. Thus, the current practice is a generous buyback of hundreds of millions per quarter and steady annual dividends. The policy is aimed at increasing shareholder value and maintaining payout, which is important for long-term investors and probably reflects management's confidence in the free cash flow generated.

Risks

Johnson Controls has a strong operational foundation, but there are many factors on the horizon that can strain performance and expectations. The weakening of the global economy, especially construction projects, threatens to reduce demand for HVAC and security systems, that is, for the main source of revenue. Increased duties on components and finished products, primarily in the Chinese direction, will add to the cost, and inflation and disruptions in the supply chain, inflating prices for steel, chips and energy, risk hitting margins faster than the company has time to renegotiate contracts. Competitors are not asleep: technology companies have joined the traditional players, actively entering smart and energy-efficient buildings. In parallel, the sale of Bosch's HVAC business is underway and the portfolio is being shaken up, and the slightest mistake here can eat into the synergies and delay the effect. On top of everything lies the volatility of currencies, stricter environmental regulations, the growth of cyber threats and the likelihood of a jump in rates.

Final Thoughts

Johnson Controls remains in good shape: the company is focused on the commercial segment and is raising margins by increasing the share of services. Stable revenue, a focus on digital solutions, and a record-breaking backlog provide a solid foundation. But market multipliers are already taking these advantages into account, and the revision of forecasts and a sharp increase in quotations are holding back further upside. Against the background of supply chain risks, competition, and possible macro volatility, securities are trading close to fair value. I assign a Hold recommendation with a target price of $101–110.

data.title: FTI Consulting: Solid Operator, Fairly Priced; A Hold
data.description:
FTI Consulting, Inc. (NYSE:FCN) strikes me as one of those consulting firms you don’t hear much about until something serious goes wrong. They don’t do strategy blueprints or digital transformation fluff. They step in when there’s fraud to investigate, a lawsuit to defend, a regulator breathing down someone’s neck, or a business in freefall. It’s high-stakes work, and FTI has built a reputation for handling it quietly and well.

Let’s look at the company’s operations. These are split across five segments: Corporate Finance & Restructuring, Forensic & Litigation Consulting, Economic Consulting, Technology, and Strategic Communications. It’s a global business with a long client list, including 90% of the Fortune 100, major law firms, banks, and private equity players. It’s the kind of outfit that makes money whether the market’s up or down, as long as something’s happening.

Now, Q1 2025 didn’t blow the doors off, but it reminded me why FTI’s model works. To that end, then, let’s take a look at whether the current setup and future outlook justify leaning in or sitting tight.

How Did The Segments Perform?

One of the things I like about FTI is that its revenue mix actually gives you a read on how global business is feeling. Each segment responds to a different kind of pressure: legal, financial, reputational, and regulatory.

Starting with Forensic & Litigation Consulting (FLC), this was the clear standout in Q1, rising by 8.3% year over year. That growth is mostly a result of a steady uptick in investigations, cross-border disputes, fraud cases, and regulatory enforcement. Based on commentary, that pipeline still looks strong, so I expect this unit to stay hot into the second half.

Technology’s performance wasn’t bad—revenue declined YoY but grew QoQ, and management says that it’s expecting a fair few headwinds in the near-term. Still, the segment is also growing pretty quickly in terms of talent, which should allow us to see some more upside over the longer term.

Moving on to Corporate Finance & Restructuring. Here, revenue was very soft, falling by ~6% YoY, mostly because the restructuring boom of 2023 has tapered off. Deal flow’s dried up a bit, and fewer distressed clients are landing on FTI’s doorstep right now. We’re seeing FCN trying to backfill with turnaround and performance work, some of it tied to private equity portfolio companies, but it’s clearly a slower patch.

Economic Consulting is still trying to recover from senior personnel exits. Revenue was down over 12%, and utilization took a hit. FTI’s economic team is one of its crown jewels, so I’m watching this closely. It goes without saying that in an industry like consulting, losing senior practitioners hurts both backlog and pricing power. Still, management is confident that the new talent they’ve been recruiting to plug the gap will make up for the drastic economic loss over the medium term. Having said that, though, the timeline for full recovery here looks like a few quarters at best.

Finally, Strategic Communications showed signs of life, up 7% year over year. If you compare it to FLC, it doesn’t sound like much, but this is a segment that has been in decline for a while. They’re seeing more corporate repositioning work and cross-sell opportunities with Legal and Restructuring clients. It’s still the smallest and least predictable segment, but even modest growth is a win at this point.

What I see across all of this is a business that is in transition. Now, let me run you through the company’s finances.

Profitability, Margins & Capital Discipline

Margins, margins. Adjusted EBITDA margin came in at 12.8%, up very slightly from 12% a year ago. Operating income margin would have also ticked higher, partly thanks to the sales mix: more high-billability work from FLC and better cost containment in Tech. But it’s also because FTI made a few tough calls behind the scenes. However, it’s actually down by 21% from last year’s figure because of a $25.2 million special charge.

The special charge caught my eye because most of it was tied to severance, which tells me they’re realigning headcount where demand’s softened, particularly in Economic Consulting and Corporate Finance. Again, FTI isn’t the kind of company that swings wildly with hiring and firing, so when they make a cut of that size, it means they’re not expecting a quick snap back in those areas. It’s defensive, but smart.

The more important thing, in my view, is how disciplined the company is with capital. FTI’s not out there chasing acquisitions or burning cash. Capex is fairly modest, mostly tech-related, and they repurchased about $186 million in shares in Q1. As of April 22, 2025, the company had repurchased around $282 million worth of stock, which is very aggressive given the stock’s drawdown. To be clear, I don’t mind that. After all, the balance sheet’s clean, net leverage is pretty good, and the buybacks are well-timed after a 20% slide over the past year. They still have ~$570 million left on the authorization, so there’s room to keep that up through the year if the stock stays weak.

What to Watch

The thing is, there’s no urgent drama around FTI, and that’s part of the challenge. The company is stable, well-run, and conservatively financed, but the stock doesn’t move on quarterly beats alone. To get back into Buy territory, I need to see a few things break right in the quarters ahead.

Restructuring Activity Needs To Return Meaningfully

FTI’s Corporate Finance & Restructuring segment is built to shine in times of dislocation. When M&A is booming or distress spikes, FTI is busy advising lenders, companies, and boards. But that tailwind has been much quieter over the last few quarters, so what the company is dealing with here is a deal environment that is still very lukewarm, at best.

All the same, all this could change quickly. For example, all we need here is for corporate defaults to start ticking up or portfolio company turnarounds becoming more urgent. Given the general macroeconomic environment (including trade deals and tariffs), it’s not too far-fetched to think about. If those circumstances arise, then FTI’s restructuring and performance improvement teams can catch another wave, and that immediately changes the revenue and sentiment trajectory. For now, though, it’s just optionality.

Technology Needs to Recover Quickly

FTI’s Technology business is a quiet one, but it looks like things might be changing. Revenue was up sequentially in Q1, and management also highlighted some strong demand from global clients. These are mostly tied to compliance, information governance, and digital forensics, especially in non-litigation contexts.

On this front, there’s some positive news. The company is partnering with BigID to “empower clients to effectively manage, protect, and optimize their vast data estates. This alliance delivers tangible results in tackling critical challenges across information security, data privacy, compliance, AI initiatives and more.”

That’s a sign of increasing demand, and if that demand holds, it’s reasonable to add this segment as one of FTI’s key earnings drivers over the next few years. If we also consider it from a strategic standpoint, expanding this segment gives FTI more room to cross-sell with FLC and CF&R. I believe that the question is whether the platform can scale efficiently, or if higher demand simply brings higher costs. I’ll be watching both revenue momentum and margin progression here closely.

Economic Consulting Needs To Rebuild Momentum

Historically speaking, this business segment has been one of FTI’s most prestigious and profitable lines: a go-to unit for expert testimony in antitrust, competition, and complex litigation. But several senior-level talent left in 2024, and the business has lost some punch. Yes, there are new, exciting hires, per management’s description, but any turnaround will take more than one or two quarters.

I’d say the main watchpoint here is leadership. During the earnings call, CEO Steven Gunby said:

“...There's been an additional development since last time, which I believe in the long term is a fabulous thing that is adding to the near-term financial pressure, which is that this business has since we last talked, focused an enormous amount of attention on replenishing talent...

And important, we've had enormous success in that endeavor. We have already been able to attract an unbelievable set of academic affiliates and new arrivals, many more and unbelievable talent more than we expected a couple of months ago. And these are folks who bring expertise across antitrust, we have some additions to our financial economics business and across key industries, which is health care, finance, TMT, digital assets, AI. If you look at their website, and I encourage you to do so and see the resumes, you'll see these are people with tremendous academic credentials. But in addition, in many cases, they have served previously in senior roles in government, including the FTC the FCC and the SEC.

…I think in line of this group, there might even be a couple of people who could be potential future Nobel prize winners. So I hope you got from my tone of voice that we're excited about these additions. If I step back from those additions, what they do is they enhance what has always been the case, my powerful confidence, my strong confidence in the medium-term prospects for this business. This is a great business with terrific people in it.”

It sounds all well and good when management is this excited about new hires. Still, the important questions we should be considering here are things like: Can they (new hires) rebuild the business book with institutions that rely on top-tier economic modeling? If they can, then this segment could go from a minor drag to a differentiator.

Strategic Communications Needs To Justify Its Seat At The Table

StratCom is the smallest and least consistent of FTI’s five segments, but it’s trying to turn a corner. As I mentioned earlier, Q1 2025 was the first quarter of year-over-year growth in a while. That’s good, and it shows that there’s some momentum in capital markets, crisis management, and internal change communications.

We need to see CommTech, corporate repositioning, and ESG messaging driving demand related to M&A or restructuring. That’s the primary growth engine that will make StratCom start to matter more, but it needs to prove consistency. One soft quarter, and it’s back to being a rounding error. I’m watching for multiple quarters of growth here before treating it as a real growth lever.

Valuation & Market Positioning

I'm neutral on valuation. The business is stable, profitable, and conservatively run. But unless Q2 or FY2026 brings an acceleration in growth or an unexpected event, I don't see the valuation moving north significantly. Now, let’s look at the multiples.

FTI currently trades at ~22× trailing P/E, and the forward multiple is in the same ballpark. So, it’s comfortably within the range (13–25x) of high-end professional services firms. On the EV/EBITDA side, it sits at about 14x, which is slightly below its 3–5-year average (15–16x).

Also, the company’s EV/Sales ratio is ~1.6x. That’s a fairly positive discount compared to the sector median, and I’d say it reflects a mid-market valuation for a business with YoY organic revenue decline but strong recurring revenue figures. Of course, those multiples modestly lower than that of certain peers, but they’re consistent with the company’s steady style.

If we look at everything outlined above holistically, it’s obvious that the market already values FTI as a reliable, well-run consultancy, not a growth rocket. The stock is down ~14% year-to-date, so it’s back to more reasonable levels if we compare it to the cyclical highs of the last year or so. Remember that the drop also partly reflects softer segments like Economic Consulting and Corporate Finance.

Still, margins are improving, and segments like FLC and Technology are getting stronger. At current multiples, you’re essentially getting a solid advisory firm at a fair price.

Risks

In my opinion, FTI’s biggest risk in the near term is talent churn. This is a people business, and when senior consultants leave, like we’ve seen in Economic Consulting, revenue follows. Replacing high-billability talent takes time, and if departures continue, it could pressure both margins and client relationships.

We can also consider cyclical exposure. Segments like Corporate Finance & Restructuring rely on deal flow or distress cycles. What this means is that if bankruptcies stay muted and M&A doesn’t pick up, those units may stagnate through year-end.

Lastly, there’s always the risk of concentration. For a consulting firm like FTI, projects tend to be large and episodic. So we may see a few big wins juice up a quarter’s results, but the reverse is also true; one delayed or lost engagement can swing results more than expected.

Investor Takeaway

FTI Consulting is doing what it’s supposed to do: managing through uneven demand, protecting margins, and staying disciplined on capital. The business is solid, the balance sheet is clean, and there’s no real red flag in the numbers. It’s a balanced setup.

On the flip side, though, I can’t say that the stock screams opportunity. At ~22x forward earnings and ~14x EBITDA, it’s trading in line with its historical range. That’s fair for what it is: a steady, high-trust advisory business with moderate growth and occasional upside from volatility in the world.

This is a hold. I like the company. I respect the management. However, unless we get a stronger macro tailwind or segment-level acceleration, I don’t see a near-term rerating.

data.title: 4 Of The Hottest Great American SWANs For The 4th Of July
data.description:
Today, I wanted to provide readers with a special July 4th SWAN Edition.

In case you aren’t familiar with the SWAN term, it stands for “sleeping well at night”.

Over the years (15 on Seeking Alpha), I’ve used the SWAN term frequently to describe some of the best “stress-free” investments to own.

And to qualify as a SWAN, the company must have undeniable blue-chip attributes such as:

  • Proven management
  • Track record of deploying capital to create shareholder value
  • Strong balance sheet
  • Good corporate governance

As in all SWAN recommendations, I like to see consistent earnings because they’re a proxy for cash flow. I’ve found that paying attention to profit margins is one of the best ways to help me avoid pitfalls and to buy shares in high-quality stocks cheaply.

Buying earnings cheaply is one of the best ways to grow your nest egg and is the best stress-free route to investment success.

So, in honor of Independence Day, I decided (with the help of Kody Kester) to put together a list of 4 Great American SWANs.

I hope you enjoy the article and I look forward to your comments below.

Realty Income (O): The Great American Dividend Machine

Whether you love or hate Realty Income, there’s no denying the fact that this REIT delivers on its promise of paying predictable monthly dividends.

While others have tried to replicate the business model by trying to entice investors into the monthly dividend mantra, they’ve failed because they didn’t have the “wide moat” attributes associated with Realty Income’s highly sustainable business model.

For example, Gladstone Commercial (GOOD), a weaker net lease REIT peer, that also pays monthly dividends, was forced to cut its dividend because of eroding earnings driven by a riskier balance sheet and higher cost of capital.

EPR Properties (EPR) is another net lease REIT peer that didn’t have the same diversification attributes as Realty Income and was forced to suspend its dividend during COVID-19 because of its elevated concentration of movie theaters within the portfolio.

As the largest net lease REIT in the world, scale certainly has advantages for Realty Income.

The S&P 500 constituent has over 15,600 properties spread across 50 states, Europe, and the United Kingdom, which provides the company with meaningful operator and geographic diversification.

Many consider scale a disadvantage given the larger denominator of assets under management; however, the benefits of scale can be best illustrated by examining the impressive earnings history that includes 98.2% average occupancy since 2010 and 29 out of 29 years of positive total operational returns.

Those who criticize Realty Income for its size seem to forget the scale advantage, which is a key driver of the successful dividend record in which the company has paid and increased dividends for 30 years in a row.

Of course, scale is not possible with judicious capital management practices.

Realty Income has one of the best balance sheets in the entire REIT sector, rated A3 (Moody’s) and A- (S&P), with solid leverage metrics, including 5.4x net debt to EBITDAre, 4.7x fixed charge coverage, and 35% net debt to total enterprise value.

Although Mr. Market views Realty Income as just another net lease REIT, I view the company through a different lens.

As seen below, weighing 50% for 2025 and 2026 is how I arrive at a fair value of $71 a share (19% discount from the current $58 share price).

This could generate a 29% total return through June 2026 and an 11% annual total return potential over the next five years, making Realty Income an easy SWAN!

Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL): A Dividend Growth All-Star In The Making

Next up on our list is none other than the mega-cap stock, Alphabet. GOOGL boasts a market cap exceeding $2 trillion, and it's not hard to see why. Its platforms are used daily by billions of users.

Why are we confident that the tech juggernaut has plenty of room to run higher in the years to come? Well, GOOGL is positioning itself to establish or maintain itself as a leader in many industries set for substantial growth.

One big picture growth avenue is the global digital advertising market. Greater availability of smartphones around the world and rising social media usage are secular trends that are expected to drive growth in this vertical. That's why The Business Research Company anticipates that the digital advertising industry will compound from $734.2 billion in 2024 revenue to top $1.4 trillion in 2029.

In other words, simply maintaining its share in this market could generate double-digit percentage annual revenue growth for GOOGL in what is currently its core business. From our perspective, the company's platforms, like Google Search and YouTube, are more than capable of doing so.

GOOGL isn't just standing idly by as the leading digital advertising company, either.

As I detailed last month, the company rolled out Gemini 2.5 in Q1 2025. CEO Sundar Pichai noted that active users in AI Studio and Gemini API were up 200% since the start of 2025 alone.

The launch of AI Overviews in 2024 is also helping GOOGL to remain competitive. Despite competition from chatbots, this is what propelled the company's Google Search business to grow search queries and revenue at a healthy rate in Q1 2025.

Growth in cloud computing figures to be another major growth driver for GOOGL. Fortune Business Insights projects that the global cloud computing industry revenue will compound by 16.6% annually from $781.3 billion in 2025 to $2.3 trillion by 2032. That's thanks to increased AI penetration and the ongoing global shift to cloud spending.

Google Cloud put up impressive growth in Q1 2025. Continued capex allocation and the complementary Wiz acquisition should deliver further growth for GOOGL.

By themselves, these are meaningful and immediate growth catalysts for the company. Yet, fellow SA analyst Oliver Rodzianko also thinks Waymo could grow to become a key component of GOOGL over the next 10 to 15 years.

For these reasons, we think the FAST Graphs analyst consensus for 11.4% annual diluted EPS growth off the 2024 base through 2027 is realistic.

GOOGL's balance sheet is also quite vigorous. The company earns an AA+ credit rating from S&P with a stable outlook.

These factors put GOOGL in an excellent position to become a major dividend growth stock over the long haul. This is because the payout ratio is going to be in the high-single-digit percentage range in 2025. That makes up for a 0.5% starting yield, which is less than the S&P 500 index's 1.2% yield.

GOOGL's shares are also a solid value. At the current $180 share price (as of July 3rd, 2025), the stock is trading at a forward 12-month P/E ratio of 18.2 (50% weight for 2025 and 50% for 2026).

This is well under the 10-year average P/E ratio of 24.9 per FAST Graphs and 19% below our fair value P/E ratio of 22.4 ($221 fair value estimate).

If GOOGL meets growth expectations and returns to fair value, it could have a 24% upside by the end of June 2026 and generate 13% annual total returns through 2030 (all info in this subhead was from sources noted above and accompanying hyperlinks).

Globe Life (GL): A Future Dividend Aristocrat

Our third pick for today is the insurance holding company, Globe Life. GL mostly provides lower-middle-income to middle-income consumers with life and supplemental health insurance policies. This is done through direct-to-consumer channels, as well as independent and exclusive agents.

For a better idea of GL's size and scale, it had $17.2 billion of policies in force as of March 31st, 2025. The company's Life segment generated nearly three-quarters of overall TTM profitability. The remaining quarter of TTM segment profitability came from the Health and Investment segments.

Moving forward, GL has a few levers that it can pull to fuel growth. Co-CEO Matt Darden noted in his opening remarks during the Q4 2024 Earnings Call these growth catalysts.

For one, there's a major opportunity to reach the large pool of underinsured or uninsured people in these marketplaces. As GL reaches these people through its various channels, we believe it can pick up more policyholders over time.

Then there's also the fact that the monthly premiums for GL's policies tend to be in the $30 to $60 range. Coupled with the peace of mind that these policies provide to policyholders, this routinely leads to high retention rates. On this note, more than 90% of the company's premium revenue was generated from policies sold in previous years.

It gets better, too. These affordable monthly premiums give GL some room for premium hikes. That should be another tailwind for the company to produce growth.

Finally, GL is committed to enhancing shareholder returns through its buyback program. Each year, the company allocates hundreds of millions of dollars to share buybacks, retiring a few percent of its share count.

These elements explain why the FAST Graphs analyst consensus is for diluted net income per share to rise by 10.2% annually through 2027 off the 2024 base of $12.37.

GL's 0.9% dividend yield may be modest, but don't sell it short. Like GOOGL, the company's payout ratio is set to be in the high single-digit percentage range for 2025. This is why we think GL appears poised to build on its 20-year dividend growth streak for the foreseeable future, potentially becoming a Dividend Aristocrat in 2030.

GL also benefits from an A credit rating from S&P with a stable outlook.

The insurer also looks to be a bargain. From the current $121 share price, the stock is priced at a forward 12-month P/E ratio of just 8.5 (50% weight for 2025 and 50% weight for 2026).

That's materially less than the 10-year average P/E ratio of 13.9 per FAST Graphs and a 24% discount to our fair value P/E ratio of 11.1 ($159 fair value).

If GL reverts to fair value and live up to the growth consensus, it could have a 33% upside ahead through June 2026 and a 14% annual total return potential by the end of 2030 (unless otherwise sourced or hyperlinked, all details in this subhead were from Globe Life's Q1 2025 Investor Update Presentation).

Thermo Fisher Scientific (TMO): A Business Powering Scientific Progress

Last but not least, we have Thermo Fisher Scientific. This company is a leading player in the life sciences industry, generating $42.9 billion in total revenue over the last four quarters.

That revenue was also geographically diversified, with about half coming from North America and the other half from Europe, Asia-Pacific, and the rest of the world.

TMO provides a variety of products and services to pharma and biotech customers, academic and government customers, and diagnostics and healthcare customers. These include reagents, instruments, software, consumables, diagnostic test kits, and lab equipment.

In other words, TMO makes the groundbreaking research and development being carried out by its customers possible. As the global economy grows and the population ages, demand for medicines and vaccines will likely continue to rise.

An investment in TMO is a bet that this higher demand for healthcare will be reflected in higher R&D spending from pharmaceutical companies. This should help the company's revenue and adjusted EPS to climb higher. As a result, the FAST Graphs analyst consensus is that adjusted EPS will grow by 9.7% annually off the 2024 base of $21.86 through 2027.

TMO's 0.4% dividend yield doesn't stand out. However, the payout ratio is positioned to be around 8% in 2025. That should give TMO the flexibility to extend its 8-year dividend growth streak.

The company also has an A- credit rating from S&P on a stable outlook.

TMO's shares are an enticing value, too. At the current $429 share price, the stock is trading at a forward 12-month P/E ratio of 18.3 (50% weight for 2025 and 50% for 2026). This is beneath the 10-year average P/E ratio of 22.2 per FAST Graphs and 18% under our fair value multiple ($521 fair value estimate).

If TMO matches growth expectations and returns to fair value, it could post a 22% total return over the next 12 months and have a 13% annual total return potential through 2030 (unless otherwise sourced or hyperlinked, all info in this subhead was according to Thermo Fisher Scientific's Annual Shareholder Meeting Presentation and Thermo Fisher Scientific's 10-K Filing).

Risks To Consider

O, GOOGL, GL, and TMO are businesses with robust fundamentals. Even so, they all have risks that could alter the investment thesis.

O's key risks include potential defaults from major tenants and unfavorable economic or demographic trends in key markets.

The REIT balances the first risk through tenant diversification. However, if any key tenants encounter financial difficulties, it could take time to find a viable substitute. In the meantime, that could weigh on O's financial results.

As I (Kody) outlined in a recent article, the REIT derived 36.9% of its total annualized base rent from its top five markets (page 51 of 59 of O's Q1 2025 10-Q Filing). Any trends, like a lack of economic growth or population loss, may impact fundamentals via reduced occupancy and market rents in such markets.

Pertinent risks for GOOGL include a potential cyberbreach, revenue cyclicality, and significant competition.

A widespread cyber breach could jeopardize the sensitive data of the company's users and advertisers. Such an occurrence could lead users/advertisers to flock to competitors.

It could also cause massive lawsuits/fines to be brought against GOOGL. Much of the company's revenue is derived from digital advertising. While generally not as vulnerable to recession as traditional advertising, GOOGL would likely still be impacted via a temporary deceleration in revenue and diluted EPS growth.

Lastly, the company seems to be executing well to emerge as a winner in the ongoing AI arms race. If that doesn't prove to be the case, however, GOOGL's growth profile could be damaged.

GL's major risks include underwriting and claims risks, liquidity risk, and potential data breaches.

The company must walk the tightrope as an insurer. GL has to balance pricing its products high enough for the assumed risk, while also remaining competitive. If it prices excessively, the company risks losing out to competition.

If it prices inadequately, a surge in claims from natural disasters or pandemics could pressure profitability. In a worst-case scenario, claims to be paid out could exceed its insurance float.

Finally, like GOOGL, GL also has a substantial amount of sensitive data on its IT networks. If this is compromised via a cybersecurity breach, that could be very costly to the company in terms of lawsuits and damage to its brand image.

Notable risks for TMO include a slowdown in biopharma spending and industry competition. Any potential pharmaceutical reform could result in reduced industry spending, which could trickle down to the company as a consequence.

TMO also must continue to innovate and work to provide the products and services that clients demand. If it fails to do so, it could lose market share to competitors like Danaher (DHR) and Agilent Technologies (A).

Summary: Four Great American Companies With Potentially Explosive Upside

In our view, the four companies highlighted today are of world-class quality. O, GOOGL, and TMO each earn 13/13 Ultra SWAN quality ratings in the Zen Research Terminal. GL is no slouch, either, with a 12/13 Super SWAN quality rating.

The four provide a blended dividend yield of approximately 1.8%, which sits moderately above the S&P 500 index's 1.2% yield.

The blended average payout ratio in the mid 20% range is also well below the mid 60% range that rating agencies think is sustainable for their industries. That's why we believe that each of these companies is well-positioned for more dividend growth in the years ahead.

The four businesses are financially sound, too.

Each company has an A or AA-rated balance sheet from S&P. Overall, the average risk of bankruptcy over the next 30 years for the group is estimated at just 1.5%, per The Zen Research Terminal.

Best of all, we think the stocks trade at valuations that offer sizable 12-month upside potential. To this point, shares are priced 18% to 24% discounts to our fair value estimates.

If they revert to fair value and match growth expectations, each could generate a 22% to 33% total return through June 2026. Over the next five years, the annual total return potential remains strong as well, varying from 11% for O to 14% for GL.

Thank you for reading, and God Bless America.

Happy SWAN Investing!

data.title: Brady Corporation: Steady Growth, Strong Margins, And Strategic Resilience
data.description:
Executive Summary

Brady Corporation (NYSE:BRC) is a leading manufacturer and supplier of identification solutions and workplace safety products with a broad international footprint. The company has demonstrated steady revenue and earnings growth, strong profitability, and a disciplined capital allocation. Recently, Brady posted record adjusted EPS benefitting from organic sales growth in the Americas and Asia and accretive acquisitions while managing macro headwinds in Europe and currency pressures. With a trailing twelve-month (TTM) revenue of $1.46 billion and net income of $194 million (net margin 13.35%), Brady’s balance sheet remains strong—net debt is modest, cash generation is robust, and the company has continued a 39-year streak of consecutive annual dividend increases. Although facing uncertainties around global tariffs and soft industrial demand in some regions Brady’s diversified business, operational efficiencies, and disciplined M&A provide a resilient foundation. Shares trade at 17.1× trailing earnings, 2.3× sales, and 2.8× book with a 1.4% dividend yield and a payout ratio of just 24%. Compared to similar companies Brady trades at a premium on profitability but remains more conservatively managed and less leveraged, supporting a positive long-term outlook.

Company Overview and Background

Brady Corporation, founded in 1914 and headquartered in Milwaukee is a global leader in identification solutions (IDS) and workplace safety (WPS). Its broad portfolio includes safety signs, labels, floor-marking tape, RFID/barcode scanners, lockout/tagout devices, software for safety compliance, printing systems, and specialty labels used across manufacturing, healthcare, chemicals, automotive, energy, and government. Products are distributed via direct sales, distributors, and digital channels, serving customers in over 100 countries. As of July 2024, Brady employed approximately 5,700 people worldwide. The company’s approach blends organic innovation with strategic acquisitions, recently acquiring Gravotech and Funai’s Microfluidic Solutions business to enhance its direct part marking and inkjet technology capabilities. Brady's decentralized manufacturing model, with most production close to the point of sale, enables flexibility and mitigates tariff impacts.

Strategic Position and Future Outlook

Brady's growth strategy is anchored by three pillars. Organic sales growth through R&D, targeted M&A in adjacent technologies, and operational efficiency. Over the past year, organic sales in the Americas and Asia grew 5.4% offsetting a weakening in Europe and Australia (where sales declined 5.4% due to macroeconomic conditions). Recent acquisitions of Gravotech and Funai have broadened Brady's offering in industrial part marking and inkjet, building out its portfolio to serve both legacy and emerging markets.

The company's R&D investment has ramped up with $19.2 million spent in Q3 2025 (up 8.5% YoY) fueling new product launches such as the i6100 industrial desktop label printer and the HH86 RFID reader—designed for rugged high-volume environments. Management has highlighted strong growth in specialty identification and optical reader technologies, with particular strength in Asia (nearly 23% organic sales growth in Q3 2025). While Europe remains challenged Brady has taken proactive cost actions and expects stabilization and eventual improvement as industrial conditions recover.

Looking forward, management is guiding for full-year adjusted diluted EPS of $4.48–$4.63 low-single digit organic sales growth and ongoing investment in product development and operational efficiency. The diversified revenue base (52% US, 30% Europe, 8% Asia, 10% Australia/other Americas) and the company’s flexible manufacturing footprint help manage risks around tariffs and global supply chains.

Financial Health

Brady maintains a strong financial position underpinned by healthy cash flows, low leverage, and disciplined capital management: Their revenues were $1.46 billion up 11.4% YoY in the most recent quarter, with acquisitions contributing 10.5% and organic growth of 1.6% (Income Statement). Operating Margin is 18.6% with adjusted operating income up 20.2% in the Americas/Asia segment in the last quarter. Total debt/total capitalization is just 13.8%; net debt is effectively minimal with $152 million in cash and $162 million in debt. In Q3 2025, Brady repurchased $33.2 million in shares and paid $44.5 million to shareholders via dividends and buybacks. The dividend yield is 1.41% with a low payout ratio (23.6%), and the company recently marked its 39th consecutive annual increase (Statistics). Management’s conservative approach ensures financial flexibility to pursue strategic growth and weather macro shocks.

Brady’s operating and net margins (18.6% and 13.4%) are industry-leading, well above those of their peers except Ennis which also maintains a high net margin (10%). Brady's ROE (17.8%) and ROA (10.2%) are again above their industry peers reflecting its efficient operations and prudent capital management. Brady posted robust revenue growth (11.4% YoY), outpacing all comparable firms. The company trades at a premium (P/E 17.1×, EV/EBITDA 11.4×), reflecting its higher profitability and lower risk. Peers like Cimpress and Deluxe are cheaper on a P/E and EV/EBITDA basis but in my opinion carry much higher leverage and weaker returns. It appears that Brady’s leverage is conservative with debt/equity sitting at 13.8% in comparison to its peers which are considerably higher. Its dividend yield is lower than its peers but the low payout and buyback activity may suggest capacity for future increases. Its 39-year record of dividend growth is unmatched among peers.

Key Risks and Challenges

Brady is exposed to incremental tariffs on certain imported products (~$3M expense in Q3 2025), though mitigated by geographic manufacturing diversity. Further escalation in trade tensions could affect input costs or demand, but management has demonstrated agility in shifting sourcing and pricing. Europe and Australia remain soft industrial markets impacting organic growth. Prolonged weakness could dampen results though cost actions have helped offset revenue declines. While recent acquisitions are accretive and synergistic, integration risks remain, especially as the company expands its technological portfolio. Like any other global company, Brady faces FX risk and inflationary pressures.

Conclusion

In my opinion, Brady Corporation stands out as a high-quality industrial company combining strong organic growth, high profitability, strong and disciplined capital allocation and a stable balance sheet. The company's operational discipline and diversified global footprint enable it to navigate cyclical headwinds and capitalize on emerging opportunities such as specialty identification, RFID, and industrial printing. Brady's financial results are strong, and many metrics are above peer averages. They have strong margins, high returns and prudent leverage. Although shares trade at a premium to some more leveraged peers, there is some justification for Brady’s stability, dividend track record, and growth prospects. I believe that the risk profile is low, and the company has multiple levers such as organic growth, M&A, efficiency, and capital return to drive shareholder value. For long-term investors who seek a mix of defensiveness and steady compounding in the industrial technology industry, I believe that Brady merits a positive outlook.

data.title: Up To The Task Of A Twisting And Turning Market
data.description:
By Eli Pars, CFA

Review

There’s certainly been no shortage of volatility in the markets this year—to both the upside and the downside. And the second quarter seemed to last a lot longer than three months! The quarter kicked off with President Trump’s “Liberation Day” announcement on April 2, catching investors off guard and sending markets into a tailspin. However, as investors digested details of the US government’s tariff proposals and the Trump administration refined what it was seeking, markets climbed back up. Even the outbreak of war between Iran and Israel couldn’t slow the global equity market down for long, and the MSCI World Index gained 9.75% for the year-to-date.

The global convertible market has more than kept pace with the global equity market so far this year, with the FTSE Global Convertible Index rising 11.17% through June. This year-to-date strength builds on the year’s first quarter, when convertible securities also outperformed equities, and global markets outperformed the US across both asset classes.

Convertibles don’t usually capture 115% of an equity move of this magnitude (up ~10%), as they have so far this year. This capture seems driven in part by a reversal of the Magnificent 7-type outperformance of mega-cap equities in recent years. (Broad equity benchmarks include greater representation to mega-caps than the convertible market; our whitepaper, “The Case for Strategic Convertible Allocations,” explores the market-cap differences between equities and convertibles—and the impact on performance—at greater length.)

After outperforming the FTSE Global Convertible Index in the first quarter, the fund trailed in the second quarter, even though it posted a robust gain in absolute terms. An underweight to Europe and an overweight to Chinese consumer names—both of which have helped in the past—hindered relative performance during the second quarter. As active managers, we continually assess our holdings and believe our positioning still makes sense as we look forward.

Issuance

Global convertible issuance surged in June with $26.1 billion in new convertibles coming to market, bringing quarterly issuance to $47.1 billion and year-to-date issuance to nearly $70.5 billion. We continue to forecast a strong new issue calendar for the remainder of the year.

As we have noted in past commentary, the pace of issuance could be more measured if equity markets stay volatile, but we believe the interest-rate environment continues to incentivize issuers to choose convertibles over nonconvertible debt to manage borrowing costs. Over recent quarters, the participation of investment-grade companies and greater regional diversification have energized the convertible market, and we would welcome a continuation of these trends.

Year-to-Date Global Convertible Issuance ($ bil)

Positioning

Calamos Global Convertible Fund (CXGCX) favors companies that offer a healthy measure of downside risk mitigation and upside equity exposure. We have maintained a significant overweight to consumer discretionary companies, as well as an aforementioned overweight to Chinese issues. These overweights are driven by convertible structures and equity valuations—in other words, by bottom-up security selection, not macro calls. On average, the fund’s portfolio has a slightly higher delta, but what we believe is a better risk-reward profile.

Conclusion

While the S&P 500 flirts with new highs, multiple potential sources of volatility could materialize in the coming months. Whether it is tariff deadlines, war in the Middle East, or the sale or ban of TikTok, plenty of things may stir the pot.

We’re ready for more twists and turns, but this is an environment that we believe plays to our experience and strengths. We’ve invested through many periods of turmoil and believe there is always opportunity.

data.title: Subsea 7: Margins Still Rising
data.description:
Subsea 7 S.A. (OTCPK:ACGYF)(OTCPK:SUBCY) has been benefiting from continued positive mix effects related to the age and risk of projects, as well as picking up new significant orders that will be beefing up the backlog in the coming quarter. Subsea 7 has relatively outperformed Saipem SpA (OTCPK:SAPMF), reflecting our belief that in the merger between them, the Subsea 7 shareholders are getting the better deal. In all, strategic considerations for oil remain an important driver and the backlog is likely to remain robust, with margin lift likely on the current backlog. We think there are real benefits to the merger, and we don't mind the outlook too much, but we are aware of the risks to the general oil market and the sustainable generation of particularly greenfield backlog. The price has gone up a lot since we started covering the company, and it is cyclical.

Latest Results

As a reminder, Subsea 7 is an EPC company that does development projects offshore to develop oil and gas assets, particularly subsea infrastructure and pipe laying projects for offshore oil and gas.

Revenues are clearly up as project deliveries proceed. EBITDA is growing ahead of revenue nicely on a couple of factors.

  • The later backlog, quoted after 2022, includes indexing clauses that have increased the profitability of these quoted EPC projects. Recall that Subsea 7 takes the risks associated with delivering a project profitably, but indexing in the contracts has been introduced since the 2022 inflation period started which has reduced risk and meant that more recent quoted projects are liquidating more profitably than earlier projects. The mix is skewing towards newer projects.
  • The mix is also skewing towards brownfield projects, which are more incremental, less risky, and therefore more profitable than greenfield projects.
  • Moreover, the mix is skewed towards projects that are further along in their delivery, where the later stages of projects usually liquidate more profitably as the foundational and more intensive work is already done.

They are still winning considerable new business since the close of Q1. Just in May, the company announced three major projects that are valued ahead of the $1.5 billion mark for the backlog. We expect this to mean solid book-to-bill ratios, likely around the 1x mark.

Bottom Line

As mentioned, Saipem had a lower multiple at the moment when the merger between them and Subsea 7 was announced. However, the merger stipulated that the merged company would be owned 50:50 by each company's shareholders. This meant that Subsea 7 shareholders were getting more ownership of their EBITDA. We felt this was unfortunate for Saipem which is experiencing an inflection in profitability. Saipem climbed around 12% versus more than 25% for Subsea 7 shareholders since the merger announcement, both reflecting the better fleet economics that will come from a combined entity, but the additional growth in Subsea 7 likely reflects the quality of the deal for them. We felt that the value given to Subsea 7 shareholders was about worth a control premium, so the more than 10% relative outperformance reflects quite a lot of the value that was embedded in those shares at that point, meaning the relative value between the stocks has closed a bit since we discussed the matter.

The question then is on earnings direction. Saipem and Subsea 7 are quite similar businesses, so the matter of the positive mix effects means that the earnings case for these businesses on an underlying basis is quite good. However, we wonder about the sustainable development of greenfield demand given that oil prices have come down. $60 per barrel is often the breakeven of offshore developments in most places. The really big projects that came in lately were brownfield, meaning that there is some reticence about sustained high oil prices particularly as OPEC+ is coming back with their supply. On the discussed mix benefits, margins are expected to improve, but sales aren't suspected to be rising that much.

The margin improvements are important and will be sustained for as long as the current backlog is in play. But we know that this industry is cyclical and therefore aren't eager to jump on at this exact moment.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: French Economy Under Significant Strain With No Signs Of Recovery
data.description:
By Charlotte de Montpellier

France lags behind its European peers

While some eurozone economies performed surprisingly well in the first half of 2024, France continues to disappoint. The latest industrial production figures confirm the ongoing deterioration in economic momentum.

In May 2025, manufacturing output declined by 1.0% month-on-month, following a revised 0.7% drop in April. Total industrial production fell by 0.5%, after a 1.4% contraction the previous month. These figures came in well below expectations. Over the past three months, manufacturing output is now 0.4% lower than during the same period in 2024.

Most industrial sectors saw declines in May, with the exception of transport equipment (aerospace, shipbuilding, rail) and electricity production. Construction output also fell by 0.5% for the second consecutive month. Over the last three months, construction activity is down 3.8% year-on-year.

These figures paint a worrying picture of the French industrial sector and suggest that the economy is under significant strain. We now expect GDP to contract by 0.1% quarter-on-quarter in the second quarter.

No signs of a turnaround

What’s more concerning is the lack of any meaningful recovery signals. Business sentiment in the industrial sector weakened further in June and remains subdued across the broader economy. With confidence low and demand expectations muted, a rebound in industrial output looks unlikely in the near term.

Several headwinds are weighing on the outlook:

  • Trade uncertainty: With the 9 July deadline looming, there is still no clarity on potential US tariffs on European goods. Exporters remain in a wait-and-see mode, delaying investment and expansion plans.
  • Euro appreciation: Since the start of the year, the euro has appreciated by 5% in nominal effective terms (against 41 key trading partners), 4.3% in real terms, and 13.5% against the US dollar. This is eroding export competitiveness and weighing on production.
  • Geopolitical tensions: Global uncertainty continues to dampen business confidence.
  • Domestic fiscal uncertainty: France is entering a difficult budget negotiation phase. A significant fiscal adjustment appears inevitable, likely involving tax hikes that could affect both households and businesses. The risk of political gridlock – or even a government collapse – could push French bond spreads higher, raising financing costs for firms.

Growth to remain subdued

All in all, the French economy is facing a challenging environment with little sign of improvement ahead. We forecast GDP growth of just 0.4% in 2025, following 1.1% in 2024, with only a modest recovery to 0.8% in 2026.
